--- Page 1 ---
Causal Model-Based Reinforcement Learning for
Sample-Efficient IoT Channel Access
Aswin Arun∗, Christo Kurisummoottil Thomas†, Rimalpudi Sarvendranath∗, and Walid Saad ‡
∗Department of Electrical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Tirupati, Andhra Pradesh, India.
† Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Worcester Polytechnic Institute, Worcester, MA, USA.
† Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Virginia Tech, Alexandria, VA, USA.
Emails:{ee22b055, sarvendranath}@iittp.ac.in, cthomas2@wpi.edu, walids@vt.edu.
Abstract—Despite the advantages of multi-agent reinforcement
learning (MARL) for wireless use case such as medium access
control (MAC), their real-world deployment in Internet of Things
(IoT) is hindered by their sample inefficiency. To alleviate this
challenge, one can leverage model-based reinforcement learning
(MBRL) solutions, however, conventional MBRL approaches rely
on black-box models that are not interpretable and cannot rea-
son. In contrast, in this paper, a novel causal model-based MARL
framework is developed by leveraging tools from causal learn-
ing. In particular, the proposed model can explicitly represent
causal dependencies between network variables using structural
causal models (SCMs) and attention-based inference networks.
Interpretable causal models are then developed to capture how
MAC control messages influence observations, how transmission
actions determine outcomes, and how channel observations affect
rewards. Data augmentation techniques are then used to generate
synthetic rollouts using the learned causal model for policy
optimization via proximal policy optimization (PPO). Analytical
results demonstrate exponential sample complexity gains of
causal MBRL over black-box approaches. Extensive simulations
demonstrate that, on average, the proposed approach can reduce
environment interactions by 58%, and yield faster convergence
compared to model-free baselines. The proposed approach inher-
ently is also shown to provide interpretable scheduling decisions
via attention-based causal attribution, revealing which network
conditions drive the policy. The resulting combination of sample
efficiency and interpretability establishes causal MBRL as a
practical approach for resource-constrained wireless systems.
I. INTRODUCTION
The proliferation of wireless sensor networks, internet of
things (IoT) devices, and machine-to-machine (M2M) com-
munication systems demands efficient medium access control
(MAC) protocols that optimize performance under dynamic
conditions [1]. While reinforcement learning (RL) [2]–[4] can
automatically learn optimal scheduling policies by explor-
ing the large state-action space of wireless environments, a
fundamental barrier to AI-native wireless systems is sample
complexity. Model-free RL requires extensive real-world inter-
actions to converge, and each sample incurs significant com-
munication overhead in resource-constrained networks. This
sample inefficiency makes practical deployment of learning-
based MAC protocols infeasible, necessitating approaches that
achieve fast convergence with minimal real-world data.
A. Related Works
In multi-agent RL (MARL) settings [5], distributed sensor
nodes can learn decentralized policies while coordinating
through control signaling with the gateway. In contrast to
model-free RL that are sample inefficient [2]–[5], model-
based RL (MBRL) [6] and [7] can significantly improve
sample efficiency, requiring a small number of samples gen-
erated through real environment interactions, by learning a
model of the environment dynamics and using it to generate
synthetic experience for policy training. Complementary RL
approaches include diffusion-based data augmentation for
replay buffers [8], [9], hierarchical MBRL with temporal
abstraction [10], and large language model (LLM)-based
knowledge integration through reward shaping [11]. However,
standard MBRL and LLM-based approaches [6], [7] often
learn black-box models whose performance are tied to the
training data and does not provide an understanding of which
variables causally influence which outcomes. This opacity
creates three fundamental challenges. First, policies learned
from black-box models may exploit spurious correlations
that fail to transfer when network conditions change. Second,
model errors accumulate over planning horizons, limiting the
effectiveness of long-horizon autonomous control. Third, the
learned policies lack explainability, making it impossible to
verify their trustworthiness before deployment, a critical con-
cern for mission-critical IoT systems. A promising approach
here is to exploit causal inference [12] to understand the
underlying causal structure of wireless dynamics, enabling
model-based agents to generalize reliably under distribution
shifts while requiring limited real-world data.
B.
Contributions
The main contribution of this paper is a novel MBRL
for device scheduling in IoT that leverages causal graphs to
explicitly represent the cause and effect relations among states,
actions, observations, and rewards. By exploiting this causal
structure, we aim to improve sample efficiency through data
augmentation using more accurate environment modeling; and
facilitate faster learning via model-based rollouts using the
causal structure. Our key contributions include:
• We formulate the wireless MAC problem for the uplink
(UL) of a multi-user IoT network using structural causal
models (SCMs) that explicitly represent the causal rela-
tionships between sensor node states, control messages,
transmission actions, observations, and rewards.
• We develop gated recurrent units (GRU) and attention-
based neural networks to learn causal dependencies mod-
eled as a SCM, with the architecture inherently providing
explainability through attention weights that reveal causal
arXiv:2511.10291v1  [cs.IT]  13 Nov 2025


--- Page 2 ---
influences on scheduling decisions. Further, we integrate
the learned causal model into a model-based RL algo-
rithm using proximal policy optimization (PPO), where
the causal model generates synthetic rollouts for policy
training.
• We analytically derive the sample efficiency gains that
can be achieved using a causal MBRL compared to tradi-
tional RL baseline, as a function of the SCM complexity
and learned model accuracy.
• Through extensive simulations1, we show that proposed
causal MBRL achieves faster convergence compared
to model-free baselines and on average better sample
efficiency via 58% reduction in environment interactions.
II. SYSTEM MODEL AND MARL FORMULATION
We consider an IoT network with U sensor nodes that com-
municate with a gateway over a shared UL channel. Each sen-
sor node aims to transmit P data packets to the gateway while
coordinating channel access through control messages. Similar
to the model considered in [13], all sensor nodes transmit their
UL data packets over the same frequency channel using time
division multiple access (TDMA) scheme. The objective is
to deliver all packets to the gateway with minimal latency,
which is critical for time-sensitive IoT applications such as
environmental monitoring, industrial control, and smart grid
systems. We consider a packet erasure channel in which the
transmitted data packets can be received in error with block
error rate (BLER) ρ ∈[0, 1]. For simplicity, we assume the
BLER is identical for all UL data transmissions. Each sensor
node has a dedicated error-free UL and downlink (DL) control
channels for exchanging signaling messages with the gateway.
We assume that control channels do not experience packet
losses and have negligible transmission delay. The gateway
implements a pre-defined MAC signaling protocol that is not
learned. Sensor nodes need to learn to communicate with
the gateway by understanding the channel access scheme and
MAC signaling protocol of the gateway with an objective to
successfully deliver the packets in minimum time possible.
In the UL, the gateway receives the scheduling requests
(SRs) from the sensor nodes, and in the DL, it sends the
scheduling grant (SG) to the specific node who has been
granted the channel access. Furthermore, in the DL, it also
sends an acknowledgment (ACK) when a packet is success-
fully received. Let ML = {0, 1, 2} denote the set of three
possible DL control messages (DCMs) from the gateway to
each node and mu
t ∈ML denote DCM sent to uth node at
time slot t. The gateway sending a null message is denoted
by mu
t = 0, releasing an SG to node u in the next time slot
is denoted by mu
t = 1, and sending an ACK is defined by
mu
t = 2. We define UL = {0, 1} as the set of UL control
messages (UCMs), where nu
t ∈UL represents the UCM at
time t from node u. The gateway will interpret nu
t = 1 as
receiving scheduling request and nu
t = 0 as no request.
1Due to space constraints, the evaluation of generalizability is omitted and
will be presented in a journal extension.
At each time step t, for each learner u, the environment
delivers a scalar observation ou
t
∈OU = [0, P] describ-
ing the number of packets that remain in the learner’s UL
transmit buffer. Here, P is the transmit buffer capacity, and
all packets are assumed to be of the same size. Similarly,
at each time step, the gateway receives a scalar observation
ob
t ∈OB = [0, L + 1] from the environment. ob
t = 0 means
that UL channel is idle, ob
t = u ∈[1, U] represents collision-
free UL transmission received from node u and ob
t = U + 1,
when collision occurred in the UL channel. The gateway also
receives the SRs {nu
t }U
u=1 from the sensor nodes via the
control channel. It selects one requesting node uniformly at
random and sends an SG to that node: mu
t = 1 for the selected
node u, and mu′
t
= 0 for all other nodes u′ ̸= u. If a node
successfully transmits data concurrently with sending an SR,
the gateway sends an ACK instead of an SG to that node, and
schedules a different node. For uth sensor node at time step
t, let au
t ∈{0, 1, 2} denotes the UL shared channel action.
Action value 0 correspond to idle, and action values 1 and 2
correspond to transmitting and deleting the oldest packet in the
buffer, respectively. We define the vector du
t = (nu
t , au
t ) as the
combined decision made by the uth sensor node. Further, the
state of each sensor node xu
t is defined, and it incorporates the
observation history, previous decisions, and received control
messages over a window of N time steps:
xu
t =

ou
t−1, du
t−1, mu
t−1, · · · , ou
t−N, du
t−N, mu
t−N

,
(1)
Similarly, the gateway maintains a state based on its observa-
tions and the control message history from all nodes:
xb
t =

ob
t−1, nt−1, mt−1, · · · , ob
t−N, nt−N, mt−N

,
(2)
where nt and mt are the vector of UCMs from all sen-
sor nodes and the vector of DCMs to sensor nodes, re-
spectively. For notational convenience, we also define the
global state xt = (xb
t, x1
t, · · · , xU
t ), global observation ot =
(ob
t, o1
t, · · · , oU
t ) and global actions dt = (d1
t, d2
t, · · · , dU
t ).
A. Multi-Agent RL Framework
Following [13], we assign a reward of rt = −1 once packets
are successfully delivered and deleted. Reward is greater than
−Tmax when all packets are delivered and deleted in less
number of steps. The total episode reward R=
T −1
P
t=0
rt, where
T ≤Tmax is the actual episode length. Given the current
state xu
t and observation ou
t , the RL agent at each sensor
node selects shared channel action and UCM according to
its policy πu(du
t | xu
t ). The objective is to learn policies
{πu(du
t | xu
t )}U
u=1 that maximize the expected cumulative
discounted reward: J(π) = Eπ [P∞
t=0 γtrt], where γ ∈[0, 1)
is the discount factor that balances immediate and future
rewards rt. Computing node policies to maximize J(π) using
standard MARL has the limitation of requiring large number
of real-world data samples, which may not be available in
practice. To overcome this challenge, we now propose a
causal MBRL approach that addresses these limitations via
explicitly capturing the causal relations among RL variables
and enabling data augmentation by generating synthetic data
samples that nearly matches the real-world samples.


--- Page 3 ---
Fig. 1: Causal Graph G for MAC Scheduling. Abstract level causal
graph is provided for simplicity of understanding. We use the fine
grained causal graph for our proposed method.
III. CAUSAL MODEL BASED WIRELESS SCHEDULING
In this section, we present a rigorous framework for learning
causal models in multi-user IoT device scheduling for channel
access. We formalize the SCM using the known causal graph
G (see Fig. 1). We then formulate a causal MBRL-based
scheduling problem that learns the structural equations (SEs)
part of causal model and optimize the scheduling policies.
A. Formal SCM Definition
We formalize the wireless scheduling environment as an
SCM M = ⟨U, V, F, P(U)⟩, where U is the set of exogenous
variables (external noise and unobserved factors), V is the
set of endogenous variables (system variables determined by
the model), F is the set of SEs defining causal relationships,
and P(U) is the probability distribution over exogenous
variables. The endogenous variables at time t, defined as Vt,
consist of agent states Xt = {x1
t, . . . , xU
t , xb
t}, observations
Ot = {o1
t, . . . , oU
t , ob
t}, UL actions Dt = {d1
t, . . . , dU
t }
(sensor node decisions), DCMs Mt = {m1
t, . . . , mU
t } (gate-
way decisions) and reward rt ∈R. The exogenous vari-
ables capture stochasticity and include state transition noise
{w1
t , . . . , wL
t , wb
t} (e.g., due to channel fading, interference),
and external environmental factors {u1
t, . . . , uU
t } . Each uu
t
corresponds to the packet arrival process that generate the
observations ou
t . Each endogenous variable v ∈Vt is defined
by a SE: v := fv(Pa(v), Uv), where Pa(v) ⊆Vt−1 ∪Vt are
the parent variables of v, i.e., all variables that directly cause
v, in the causal graph, and Uv represents the exogenous inputs
affecting v. Specifically, we have state transition equations as
xu
t+1 := f u
x (xu
t , ou
t , du
t , mu
t , wu
t ),
u = 1, . . . , U,
xb
t+1 := f b
x(xb
t, ob
t, dt, mt, wb
t),
(3)
and observation equations as
ou
t+1 := f u
o (xu
t , au
t , uu
t ),
u = 1, . . . , U,
ob
t+1 := f b
o(xb
t, dt, wb
t),
(4)
and reward equation as rt+1 := fr(xt, dt, mt, ot).
(5)
The causal relationships in the SCM are represented by a
directed acyclic graph (DAG) G = (V, E), where nodes
correspond to endogenous variables V = Vt ∪Vt+1 and
directed edges represent direct causal relationships: (vi, vj) ∈
E if vi ∈Pa(vj). Unlike knowledge graphs that represent
semantic relationships or correlative associations [14], SCM
is specifically designed to capture direct causal influences
and avoids the exponential blowup of learning unstructured
dependencies. It preserves interventional semantics, i.e., the
graph remains valid when network conditions vary, enabling
policies generalize across channel conditions. SCMs also
satisfy the Markov property, i.e., each variable is conditionally
independent of its non-descendants given its parents.
B. Problem Formulation: Causal MBRL Based Scheduling
Given G, we learn the functional forms of the SEs
in (3)-(5) using parameterized inference networks.2 For
each
endogenous
variable
vj
∈
V,
the
objective
is
to learn a conditional distribution: qϕ(vj
|
Pa(vj))
≈
p(vj | Pa(vj)), by minimizing the negative log-likelihood
−P
vj∈Vt+1 Eδ∼D[log qϕ(vj|Pa(vj))], where qϕ is parame-
terized by neural network weights ϕ, and p represents the
true conditional distribution in the environment. Here, the
expectation is over the empirical distribution p(vj | Pa(vj))
obtained using the wireless data D that involves the stat-
action-reward trajectories. Challenges here involve heteroge-
neous parent variable types and dimensions across Pa(vj),
dynamic structural changes as the causal graph evolves over
time, and complex temporal dependencies among sequential
actions. To handle heterogeneous inputs, we introduce a
variable encoding layer that maps all variables to a common
embedding space. This variable encoder is a two-layer MLP
with ReLU activation function.
Given the current-time variables Vt, we obtain the encoded
set ˆVt = {ˆsi = f(si) : si ∈Vt}. Encoders are shared
across all inference networks, enabling transfer learning. The
architecture for inference network is inspired from the causal
model discussed in [15]. For each target variable vj ∈V,
we construct an inference network f j : Pa(vj) →P(vj) that
outputs a probability distribution over vj. We partition the
parent set into state variables and action variables: Pa(vj) =
Sj ∪Aj, where Sj = Pa(vj) ∩(Xt ∪Ot) are the states and
observations and Aj = Pa(vj) ∩(Dt ∪Mt) are the actions
and control messages. This partitioning is considered because
states are processed independently, while actions are processed
sequentially to capture temporal dependencies. Further, the
integrated causal MBRL problem aims at optimizing the
causal model neural network parameters ϕ and policy model
parameters θ:
min
θ,ϕ
nepoch
X
i=1
h
L(i)
RL(θ, ϕ) + L(i)
model(ϕ)
i
,
(7)
where the RL loss L(i)
RL(θ, ϕ) = −Eπθ,qϕ
 TP
t=0
γtrt

is aver-
aged over the real-world interaction with the wireless envi-
ronment, nepoch is the number of real-world data interactions
stored in D, and the causal model loss is
L(i)
model(ϕ) =−
X
vj∈Vt+1
Eδ∼D(i)[log qϕ(vj|Pa(vj))] + λ∥ϕ∥2
2.
For
a
batch
B
⊂
D
of
size
B,
Lmodel(ϕ)
=
−1
B
P
δ∈B
P
vj∈Vt+1 log qϕ(vδ
j | Pa(vj)δ), where vδ
j is the
observed value of vj in transition δ.
2We note that constraint-based methods (PC algorithm) or score-based approaches
(greedy equivalence search, NOTEARS) could be employed in future work to infer G
from wireless data.


--- Page 4 ---
f j : Pa(vj)
encode
−−−→Pa(vj)encoded
separate
−−−−→
(
States Sj
Actions Aj
process
−−−−→hj
decode
−−−→qϕ(vj | Pa(vj)).
(6)
IV. PROPOSED SOLUTION: CAUSAL MBRL ALGORITHM
We now describe the MBRL algorithm and analyze sample
efficiency gains. We propose to solve ϕ and θ in an alternating
way via a three-phase approach executed each epoch to
optimize (7): (i) collect real environment trajectories using
πθ, (ii) periodically update the causal model parameters ϕ
using accumulated data, and (iii) optimize the policy network
parameters θ using both real and synthetic data generated
from the learned model. The detailed steps are provided in
Algorithm 1. We model the causal inference network using
attention based neural networks.
A. Causal Inference Networks
An overview of the architecture is represented by (6).
1) Value vector computation: For each state variable si ∈
Sj, we compute a contribution vector (aka value vector)
cj
i using a single layer MLP. cj
i represents the potential
contribution of state si to predicting vj.
2) Action embedding with GRU: Given encoded actions
{ˆai}ai∈PA(vj), the GRU computes the action embedding ej =
GRUj({ˆai}). From this, we derive the query vector qj =
W j
q ej + bj
q and action contribution vector cj
a = W j
aej + bj
a,
where weights W j
q , W j
a and biases are learnable parameters.
Each state variable si has a learnable key vector ki (shared
across all inference networks to ensure consistent variable
importance representations). The attention score ej
i = kT
i qj
measures compatibility between state si and query qj. We
normalize attention weights using:
αj
i =
exp(ej
i)
1 + P
sk∈Sj exp(ej
k)
, αj
a
=
1
1 + P
sk∈Sj exp(ej
k)
.
The attention weights provide interpretability of the learned
causal model. High attention weight αj
i indicates that state
si has strong causal influence on vj given the current action
context. We then compute the hidden representation of the
posterior distribution as a weighted sum of the value vectors:
hj =
X
si∈P A(vj)
αj
i · cj
i + αj
a · cj
a.
(8)
The final component maps the hidden representation to dis-
tribution parameters. The decoder Dj : Rdh →Θvj is a two-
layer MLP, where Θvj is the parameter space for the distribu-
tion of vj. For continuous variables (e.g., states, observations):
qϕ(vj | Pa(vj)) = N(µj, σ2
j ), where [µj, log σj] = Dj(hj).
We predict log σj to ensure positivity. For discrete variables
(e.g., discrete actions): qϕ(vj | Pa(vj)) = Categorical(πj)
where πj = softmax(Dj(hj)).
3) Policy update using data augmentation: We now in-
tegrate the learned causal model into a complete MBRL
framework. We leverage the learned causal model to generate
synthetic data matching real-world dynamics, reducing the
need for extensive wireless samples during training. Short
Algorithm 1: Proposed Causal Model-Based RL
1: Input: Environment with unknown dynamics; initial policy
parameters θpolicy; hyperparameters nepoch, ngraph, nround, krollout
2: Output: Optimized policy πθ
3: Initialization: Replay buffer D ←∅, causal graph G as in Fig. 1,
inference network parameters ϕ ∼N(0, 0.01), policy networks
θpolicy ∼N(0, 0.01), value network θvalue ∼N(0, 0.01).
4: for i = 1 to nepoch do
5:
Phase 1: Real Environment Interaction
6:
for each episode e = 1 to nepisodes do
7:
Initialize state x0 ∼p0(x)
8:
for t = 0 to T −1 do
9:
Sample actions: du
t ∼πu(·|xu
t ) ∀u; mt ∼πb(·|xb
t)
10:
Execute actions; observe xt+1, ot+1, rt, store in D
11:
end for
12:
end for
13:
Phase 2: Causal Model Update
14:
if i mod ngraph = 0 then
15:
for gradient step = 1 to Nmodel do
16:
Sample minibatch B ⊂D
17:
Compute Lmodel(ϕ), and update ϕ ←ϕ−ηmodel∇ϕLmodel
18:
end for
19:
end if
20:
Phase 3: Policy Optimization
21:
for j = 1 to nround do
22:
Generate synthetic data: Dsim ←∅
23:
Sample initial states {x(i)
0 }Nrollout
i=1
from D
24:
for i = 1 to Nrollout do
25:
trajectory ←krollout(x(i)
0 , π, krollout) using Algorithm 2
26:
Dsim ←Dsim∪trajectory
27:
end for
28:
Dcombined ←D ∪Dsim
29:
for ppo epoch = 1 to Nppo do
30:
Sample minibatch B ⊂Dcombined, Compute { ˆAt}
31:
Update policy: θpolicy ←θpolicy −ηpolicy∇θLPPO
32:
Update value: θvalue ←θvalue −ηvalue∇θLvalue
33:
end for
34:
end for
35: end for
36: Return: Learned policy πθ
rollouts (k ∈[3, 10]) for synthetic data reduce model error ac-
cumulation for higher accuracy but sacrifice sample efficiency,
whereas long rollouts k ∈[10, 50] improve efficiency at the
cost of compounding errors. Further, PPO is used to optimize
the network parameters because it safely handles training on
both real and simulated data through importance weighting
and clipping, which is critical for MBRL. Using both real
and simulated data, our loss function LPPO(θ) is equal to
E(xt,at)∼D
h
min
␀ρt(θ), clip
␀ρt(θ), 1 −ϵ, 1 + ϵ
 ˆAt
i
(9)
where ρt(θ) =
πθ(dt|xt)
πθold(dt|xt),
ˆAt is the advantage estimate.
The advantage function is formally defined as Aπ(xt, dt) =
Qπ(xt, dt)−V π(xt), where Qπ(xt, dt) is the expected return
when taking action dt in state xt and then following policy π,
and V π(xt) is the expected return in state xt and following
policy π. clip() function constraints ρt within {1 −ϵ, 1 + ϵ}.


--- Page 5 ---
Algorithm 2: k-Step Model Rollout
1: Initialize Dsim ←[], x ←x0
2: for τ = 0 to k −1 do
3:
du
τ ∼πu(· | xu
τ ), ∀u. // Sample actions from current policy:
4:
mτ ∼πb(· | xb
τ)
// Predict next state and observations using causal model
5:
for u = 1 to U do
6:
xu
τ+1 ∼qϕ(xu
τ+1 |Pa(xu
τ+1)), ou
τ+1 ∼qϕ(ou
τ+1 |Pa(ou
τ+1))
7:
end for
8:
rτ = fr(xτ, dτ, mτ, oτ) // Compute reward
9:
Dsim ←Dsim ∪{(xτ, dτ, mτ, rτ, xτ+1, oτ+1)}
10:
x ←xτ+1 // Update current state
11: end for
12: return Dsim
B. Theoretical Analysis
We justify sample efficiency through causal factorization.
Under standard assumptions (identifiability, bounded state,
and action spaces, Markov property, Lipschitz dynamics), the
sample complexity for causal MBRL is:
Theorem 1. For a wireless MAC system with |V| variables
(includes state, action and reward), maximum support Vmax,
causal in-degree din (maximum number of parents for a single
node in G), and a desired accuracy of the causal model ϵ > 0,
causal MBRL achieves sample complexity:
Ncausal = O
␀|V |2 · V din
max/
␀ϵ2(1 −γ)

(10)
versus black-box MBRL’s O(V |V |
max/ϵ2), yielding exponential
improvement ∼V |V|−din
max
/|V|2.
Proof: Detailed proof is omitted due to space con-
straints. The key insight is that the joint distribution over
all variables can be factorized using causal structure p(V) =
Q
vj∈V
p(vj|Pa(vj)). Each conditional p(vj|Pa(vj)) depends
only on din(vj) parents, where din(vj) ≤din ≪|V| by the
acyclic structure constraint. By standard results in learning
theory (Hoeffding’s inequality and finite hypothesis classes)
[16], [17], to learn a single conditional p(vj|Pa(vj)) with
accuracy ϵ, we require samples proportional to the support
size: Nj = O

V din(vj)
max
/ϵ2
. This is because the conditional
probability table has Vdin
max entries (one for each parent combi-
nation), each requiring O(log(1/ϵ)/ϵ2) samples for accurate
estimation. Since we must learn all |V| conditionals, and
learning one variable does not immediately improve learning
others (by causality), the total complexity is:
Ntotal =
X
vj∈V
Nj = |V| · O
Vdin
max
ϵ2

= O
|V| · Vdin
max
ϵ2

The |V|2 factor appears when accounting for variance reduc-
tion across epochs and confidence bound union over variables
(Hoeffding-style bounds) [18].
By leveraging causal structure, Theorem 1 shows that
learning complexity scales with the in-degree din rather than
the total number of variables |V|; since wireless dynamics are
sparse (few variables directly influence each outcome), this
causal factorization dramatically reduces sample requirements
compared to unstructured black-box approaches.
TABLE I: Hyperparameters and algorithm configuration
Hyperparameter
Value
Hyperparameter
Value
Learning rate α
1e−4
Discount factor γ
0.99
GAE parameter λ
0.95
Rollout horizon krollout
6
Nrollout
50
Nmodel
100
NPPO
5
Batch size
64
Episodes per epoch
20
ngraph
1
nround
4
Number of evaluation episodes
128
History N (U = 2)
3
History N (U = 1)
1
V. SIMULATION RESULTS AND ANALYSIS
We validate the sample efficiency and performance of the
proposed causal MBRL based wireless scheduling using ex-
tensive Monte-Carlo simulations. We implemented and tested
the training procedure described in Algorithms 1 and 2. We
have collected the performance, i.e., total reward R, per
episode. We start our simulations with a full buffer at the user
and repeated the training for maximum timesteps per episode
Tmax = 4, 8, 16, 32. The dimension of the variable encoder
embedding vector is chosen to be d = 64. For the inference
networks, the input dimension is 64 and the dimension of
one hidden layer dh = 128. The dimension of the query
vector is dq = 32, and the ReLU activation function is used.
The output dimension is 2 representing mean and variance
of the causal node with softmax as the activation function
and cross-entropy loss function. The policy network is similar
with input dimension 64, one hidden layer with dimension
equal to 128, and tanh is used as the activation function.
The output dimension is 3, outputting the UL shared channel
action, UCM, and the logarithm of the sum of probabilities
of channel and UL actions. Cross-entropy loss function is
employed. Training parameters are listed in Table I.
Pre-defined policy at the node: We compare the reward of
our proposed causal MBRL with the one obtained when the
nodes implement a pre-defined MAC signaling policy known
to the gateway as described in Algorithms 1 and 2 of [13].
Tabular Q-learner: We also compare with [13] that chooses
the action and uplink signaling based on Q-tables.
Sample efficiency analysis: Figs. 2a and 2b shows the
average reward as a function of the maximum timesteps per
episode Tmax for U = 1 and U = 2, respectively. They
compare the performance of the proposed causal MBRL, the
tabular Q-learning approach [13], and the pre-defined policy.
MBRL training is done for 210 and 213 episodes for U = 1
and U = 2, respectively. Furthermore, the reward shown is
averaged over 8 random seeds. For U = 1, we see that
after 210 episodes, the performance of the causal MBRL
converges to higher value than tabular Q-learning due to the
synthetic rollouts from the learned causal model. Furthermore,
tabular Q-learning requires 213 episodes to converge, in turn,
requiring a higher number of samples. Pre-defined policy
performs worse because it waits for the schedule grant, unlike
the learning approaches. For U = 2, we have shown the
performance for 213 episodes. Here, the causal MBRL is
significantly better than Q-learning. For Tmax = 32, the
reward is −13.5 and −27 for the causal MBRL and Q-
learning, respectively. Here, the pre-defined policy seems
to perform better as more number of training episodes are
required for learning approaches to converge.

8
12
16
20
24
28
32
36
-9
-8
-7

Causal MBRL (210 episodes)
Tabular Q-Learning (210 episodes)

(a) U = 1

Tabular Q-Learning (213 episodes)

(b) U = 2
Fig. 2: Causal MBRL wireless scheduling: Average episode rewards
vs Tmax showing performance across different U (BLER= 0.5).
TABLE II: Comparison of number of samples required
Nodes
Q-Learning
Causal MBRL
Efficiency
U = 1
37.5K
12.5K
66.66%
U = 2
243.1K
126.7K
47.9%
All values represent total environment interactions (real-world
samples) required to reach 95% optimal reward.
Table II compares the number of samples required. It
shows that the number of real-world samples required until
convergence for causal MBRL is 66.66% and 47.9% (and an
average 58%) less than that of tabular Q-learning for U = 1
and U = 2, respectively. This shows the superior sample
efficiency of the proposed causal MBRL method.
Fig. 3 shows average reward as a function of the training
episodes for the causal MBRL and tabular Q-learning. This is
done for Tmax = 16 and Tmax = 32. We see that the reward
increases and then saturates for both the learning approaches.
For the smaller number of episodes, the increase is higher for
the causal MBRL compared to the tabular Q-learning, which
shows that our approach learns faster with fewer samples. We
see that the reward of the causal MBRL saturates faster for
Tmax = 16 compared to Tmax = 32 as the number of steps
in each episode is smaller. However, tabular Q-learning takes
around the same number of episodes to saturate.
VI. CONCLUSION
We have introduced causal models for IoT device schedul-
ing, addressing fundamental limitations of existing MBRL
approaches. Unlike black-box neural network models, our
method discovers sparse DAGs that explicitly represent causal
dependencies between states, actions, and rewards. The result-
ing causal structure enables (i) improved sample efficiency
through reduced parameterization, (ii) better generalization
via transferable causal mechanisms, and (iii) interpretabil-
ity through attention-based causal attribution. Our attention-
based inference networks learn to understand unknown MAC
signaling protocols while coordinating channel access across
multiple agents. Our simulations validate the approach and
demonstrate significant improvements in convergence speed,
throughput, and collision avoidance compared to model-free
baselines.
0
200
400
600
800
1000
Training episodes
-25
-20
-15
-10
-5

Causal MBRL Tmax = 32

Tabular Q-Learning Tmax = 16

TS 38.321, 3GPP, 2020, Release 16.

management in wireless networks via multi-agent deep reinforcement
learning,” IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications, vol. 20, no.
6, pp. 3507–3523, Jun. 2021.
[3] Y. Yu, T. Wang, and S. C. Liew, “Deep-reinforcement learning multiple
access for heterogeneous wireless networks,” IEEE Journal on Selected
Areas in Communications, vol. 37, no. 6, Jun. 2019.
[4] H. Ye, G. Y. Li, and B. H. F. Juang, “Deep reinforcement learning for
resource allocation in V2V communications,” IEEE Transactions on
Vehicular Technology, vol. 68, no. 4, pp. 3163–3173, Apr. 2019.
[5] A. Feriani and E. Hossain, “Single and multi-agent deep reinforcement
learning for ai-enabled wireless networks: A tutorial,” IEEE Communi-
cations Surveys & Tutorials, vol. 23, no. 2, pp. 1226–1252, 2021.
[6] J. I. Park and K. W. Choi, “Model-based reinforcement learning for
wireless channel access,” in Proceedings of the IEEE 13th International
Conference on Information and Communication Technology Conver-
gence (ICTC), Jeju Island, Korea, 2022, pp. 1393–1395.
[7] C. Hasan, A. Agapitos, D. Lynch, A. Castagna, G. Cruciata, H. Wang,
and A. Milenovic, “Continual model-based reinforcement learning for
data efficient wireless network optimisation,” in Proceedings of Joint
European Conference on Machine Learning and Knowledge Discovery
in Databases, 2024.
[8] T. Gao, K. A. Neusypin, D. D. Dmitriev, B. Yang, and S. Rao, “Enhanc-
ing sample efficiency and exploration in reinforcement learning through
the integration of diffusion models and proximal policy optimization,”
arXiv preprint arXiv:2409.01427, 2024.
[9] Y. Dong and J. Zhang, “Enhancing sample efficiency in online rein-
forcement learning via policy-guided diffusion models,” in Proceedings
of the IEEE 13th International Conference on Computing and Pattern
Recognition, Oct. 2024, pp. 334–339.
[10] R. Schiewer, A. Subramoney, and L. Wiskott, “Exploring the limits of
hierarchical world models in reinforcement learning,” Scientific Reports,
vol. 14, no. 1, pp. 26856, 2024.
[11] F. Zhang, J. Li, Y. C. Li, Z. Zhang, Y. Yu, and D. Ye, “Improving sample
efficiency of reinforcement learning with background knowledge from
large language models,” IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and
Learning Systems, 2025.
[12] C. K. Thomas, C. Chaccour, W. Saad, M. Debbah, and C. S. Hong,
“Causal reasoning: Charting a revolutionary course for next-generation
AI-native wireless networks,” IEEE Vehicular Technology Magazine,
vol. 19, no. 1, pp. 16–31, 2024.
[13] A. Valcarce and J. Hoydis, “Toward joint learning of optimal MAC
signaling and wireless channel access,” IEEE Transactions on Cognitive
Communications and Networking, vol. 7, no. 4, pp. 1233–1243, 2021.
[14] C. Louizos, U. Shalit, J. M. Mooij, D. Sontag, R. Zemel, and
M. Welling, “Causal effect inference with deep latent-variable models,”
in Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 2017, vol. 30.
[15] Z. Yu, J. Ruan, and D. Xing, “Explainable reinforcement learning via
a causal world model,” arXiv preprint arXiv:2305.02749, 2023.
[16] Wassily Hoeffding,
“Probability inequalities for sums of bounded
random variables,”
Journal of the American Statistical Association,
vol. 58, no. 301, pp. 13–30, 1963.

Theory to Algorithms, Cambridge University Press, 2014.

ties,” in Summer School on Machine Learning, pp. 208–240. Springer
Berlin Heidelberg, Berlin, Heidelberg, 2003.
